questions.question: When the body is stabilized by external assistance during exercises such as a bench, it is termed a _______.
questions.explanation: Open kinetic chain exercises use external stabilization as the weight moves around the body, as seen during the bench press and lat pull down. During closed kinetic chain exercises the body moves around a fixed distal position as seen during push-ups and pull-ups.

questions.question: Which exercise would increase range of motion in the upper hamstrings?
questions.explanation: The Romanian deadlift requires hip flexion with limited knee flexion, causing the hamstrings to lengthen. The other exercises all require significant hip and knee flexion. The Romanian deadlift causes greater range of motion in the proximal hamstrings, so straight-leg stretches should also be included to ensure the distal portion is equally flexible.

questions.question: Which component of an exercise program is used to bring the body back to baseline homeostasis?
questions.explanation: An appropriate cool down will help minimize blood pooling after a high-intensity training session. A proper cool down should include full range of motion movements that mimic the exercises included in the resistance training bout to bring the body back to a pre-exercise state. The emphasis should be on fluid, continuous movements to mobilize circulating blood in venous structures and are often followed by foam rolling and static stretching.

questions.question: The principle of _________________ is defined by a new stress above what the body is accustomed.
questions.explanation: The principle of overload is defined as training stress which challenges a physiological system above the level to which it is accustomed. Intensity, duration, and rest intervals all impact overload. In the absence of adequate stress, the body will “hit a plateau” until further overload is experienced in a manner that warrants new adaptations from the body. The most common error in self-selected exercise intensity is inadequate stress.

questions.question: Which of the following muscles are targeted in the leg press?
questions.explanation: Since the hips stay flexed during the leg press it increases activation requirements in the quadriceps. Romanian deadlifts and leg curls focus on the hamstrings, glute bridges and squats emphasize the glutes, while lateral lunges and lateral squats emphasize the abductors.

questions.question: Your client fails the shoulder flexion assessment test. What muscle is tight?
questions.explanation: If a client fails to display adequate range of motion during a shoulder flexion assessment the latissimus dorsi is likely the problem. The latissimus dorsi functions to perform shoulder extension in the sagittal plane. Therefore, it will create limitations during shoulder flexion when tight.

questions.question: When designing an exercise program for a client who is focused on increasing lean mass, what is the ideal training intensity for each of the lifts to be performed?
questions.explanation: 70-85% is the ideal training intensity for lifts used to promote protein synthesis. The goal of adding lean mass requires significant muscle recruitment and anabolic hormone release, which is best achieved with sets of 8-12 repetitions. Clients looking to add lean mass and gain significant strength may benefit from 6-10 repetitions.

questions.question: Your client is jogging on the treadmill for 30 minutes. After about 6 minutes he reaches his target heart rate, which is maintained (fluctuates less than 5 beats/min) for the duration of the training. What type of training is your client performing?
questions.explanation: When a constant heart rate is achieved during aerobic training, it is classified as steady-state training. Steady-state is defined by a change in heart rate by less than 5 beats per minute. While useful for long-duration training and for acclimation for new exercisers, interval training yields better results in adaptation by volume.

questions.question: What affect does the disease sarcopenia have on the body?
questions.answer: it reduces force and power in skeletal muscle
questions.explanation: Sarcopenia is an adult-onset disease associated with physical inactivity. It represents a loss of protein from fast-twitch fibers causing a reduction in force and power within the muscle tissue. It is prevented with strength and power training regardless of age.

questions.question: When utilizing the Borg rate of perceived exertion (RPE) 6-20 scale, what number corresponds with a training zone of 60-80% HRR?
questions.explanation: Typically, a value of 12-14 on the RPE scale correlates with roughly 60-80% heart rate reserve. When using the Borg rate of perceived exertion (RPE) scale, the number 17 corresponds with “very hard.” Recall that the Borg scale has 15 levels, ranging from 6-20, which correlate with heart rates ranging from ~60-200 beats per minute (bpm).

questions.question: Clients diagnosed with type II diabetes will benefit most from what type of training due to its effects on blood glucose dynamics?
questions.explanation: Resistance training benefits clients diagnosed with type II diabetes because the muscle stimulus causes increased cellular uptake of glucose during and following exercise; reducing circulating glucose levels. Resistance training should be complemented by aerobic work as diabetics are also at high risk for heart disease.

questions.question: What is the correct course of action for a client who cannot perform any proper repetitions during normal assessments such as the push-up or pull-up protocol?
questions.answer: Select another assessment of strength they can complete safely
wrong: Require a medical referral prior to exercise
Document the findings and recommend they start with aerobic exercise only
Skip the testing until they are capable of fitness measures
questions.explanation: In many cases clients fall significantly below prior population exercise norms. Many people cannot perform body weight exercises effectively. Pull-ups are commonly impossible for a previously-sedentary male or female. If an individual fails a test without pain or injury, document the information and select a more appropriate test.

questions.question: Which is commonly used as a predictor of risk for participation in an exercise program?
questions.explanation: Body fat tests can be, in part, used as a predictor of risk due to the correlation between obesity and various diseases. Measuring body fat requires no physiological stress. Therefore, it is a resting measure much like resting heart rate and blood pressure. Concussion protocols are now common in athletic evaluations but are not warranted in health and fitness environments.

questions.question: When working with children it is particularly important to allow for adequate rest intervals and hydration due to their _______________.
questions.answer: reduced ability to regulate body temperature
wrong: lack of focus and attention to instruction
inability to use the phosphagen system
lack of coordination from immature neural function
questions.explanation: Due to a reduced ability to regulate body temperature as effectively as adults, it is important to allow adequate rest intervals and account for proper hydration when working with children. Children can generally tolerate short, intense exercise nearly as well as adults. However, they do have a reduced surface volume and immature sweat glands; which can reduce their sweating rate and negatively impact thermoregulation.

questions.question: Which of the following would be the most appropriate focus of an exercise prescription for a 10 year-old?
questions.answer: focus on full range movement and motor learning
wrong: use agility and speed training to promote coordination
questions.explanation: Exercise prescription for children should focus on motor learning and proper movement technique. General exercise recommendations for children suggest no strength training with loads greater than an 8RM (80% 1RM), with an emphasis on multi-joint activities performed for 1-2 sets. Long-duration aerobic activity is unwarranted; instead children should perform short bouts (10 minutes) of aerobic exercise at varied intensities.

questions.question: Older clients are more likely to demonstrate which movement limitation when compared to younger clients?
questions.explanation: Increasing age is associated with decreasing capabilities for spinal extension due to the progressive onset of kyphosis, and is commonly associated with osteoporosis. Commonly the body begins a forward migration as the shoulders round and the spine bends to advanced kyphotic positions. Tight hip flexors are common among all adults with the amount and type of physical activity often predicting range of motion capabilities.

questions.question: While it is important to maximize VO2 before age 30, after age 65 it is most important to ensure adequate ______.
questions.explanation: While VO2max is associated with lifespan, function and independence are associated with muscular fitness. Increased strength and power among the elderly reduce fall risk, osteoporosis and sarcopenia. Additionally, resistance training is recommended to maintain lean mass; benefiting metabolic function and reducing the risk for obesity.

questions.question: For a client with moderately elevated blood pressure, a low VO2max, and high body fat, which of the following would be most effective?
questions.answer: Cardiovascular training and reduced sodium intake
wrong: Superset training and elevated protein intake
questions.explanation: Cardiovascular training can have a positive impact on all of this client’s issues and limitations via caloric expenditure and weight loss. Reduced sodium intake as well as increased potassium can also positively impact blood pressure over time.

questions.question: The posterior pelvic tilt seen in this picture, is commonly seen in bilateral leg exercises a result of tight ____________________.
questions.explanation: The hip extensors, including the glutes and proximal insertion of the hamstrings, are often found to be inflexible. Part of the reason for this common occurrence is attributed to the increased propensity to sit for long hours with flexed knees and rounded back. Improper seated posture causes posterior pelvic migration and reduced hip extensor length. This same pelvic deviation can be seen during the leg press with tight hip extensors.

questions.question: Ankle dorsi flexion is caused by _________________.
questions.answer: a concentric contraction of the anterior tibialis
wrong: an isometric contraction of the soleus
an eccentric contraction of the biceps femoris
a concentric contraction of the gastrocnemius
questions.explanation: A concentric contraction of the anterior tibialis would cause dorsi flexion of the ankle, as seen during the recovery phase of jogging. Weakness in the anterior tibialis has also been shown to increase the prevalence of shin splints among runners and falls among older adults.

questions.question: When performing biceps and triceps exercises, what joint action should occur at the shoulder?
questions.explanation: No movement should occur in the glenohumeral joint during arm exercises. The most common "cheat" error is flexing the hips and extending the shoulders during triceps exercises, and extending the hips and flexing the shoulders during bicep exercises.

questions.question: What exercise is used to cause elevation of the shoulder complex (scapulae and humerus)?
questions.explanation: The trapezius musculature is comprised of three portions that serve distinct functions. The upper trapezius is responsible for shoulder elevation as seen during a shrug exercise, the middle trapezius aids in scapular rotation and the lower trapezius is primarily reserved for functions above 90 degrees of shoulder abduction.

questions.question: What is the natural curvature of the thoracic spine?
questions.explanation: The thoracic spine naturally maintains a kyphotic curve. A kyphotic curvature rounds outward but should not be confused with the term kyphosis, which refers to an abnormally significant curvature commonly seen among the elderly.

questions.question: What is a common error when performing an incline dumbbell press?
wrong: The arms are not fully extended at the top of the concentric phase
The hips are lifted off the bench
The wrist and elbows lose alignment
questions.explanation: Common errors during dumbbell presses are to overly-flex the elbows so the dumbbells are over the chest, not fully extending the arms to alignment over the shoulder, lifting the hips off the bench and not using a flat foot position.

questions.question: What back muscle is the prime mover for the seated row exercise as it is responsible for retraction of the scapula?
questions.explanation: Scapular retraction supersedes arm extension during the row and is further promoted through hyper-extension of the shoulder as controlled by the rhomboids. While the rhomboids do not attach to the arm they are heavily involved in rowing exercises such as the seated row, bent-over row and one-arm row.

questions.question: Which of the following exercises requires the greatest contribution from trunk stabilizers when using 80% 1RM?
questions.explanation: Due to the horizontal position of the body during the bent over row, the trunk must maintain a high level of stability from isometric muscle contractions. This partially explains why exercisers remain too vertical and have a hard time remaining in the proper position.

questions.question: The performance of dumbbell side raises occurs in what movement plane?
questions.explanation: The dumbbell side raise occurs in the frontal movement plane. The frontal plane bisects the body at the midaxillary line, splitting the body into equal front and back halves. Frontal-plane actions are performed when moving right or left and include ab/adduction exercises. To maintain muscle tension on the deltoids during the whole set, abduction should begin at about 30 degrees from the sides of the body.

questions.question: What position of the knee is necessary to fully stretch the gastrocnemius when the ankle is in full dorsi flexion?
wrong: it is not affected by the knee in dorsi flexion
questions.explanation: Since the gastrocnemius crosses the knee it must be extended to optimize the stretch. Active-isolation stretches for the calves actually use a constant contraction of the quadriceps to extend the knee along with anterior tibialis controlled dorsi flexion to optimize the gastrocnemius stretch.

questions.question: What muscles are used to globally stabilize the humerus during the bench press?
questions.explanation: The pectoralis major is the prime mover for the bench press, but the latissimus dorsi and shoulders must control the position of the humerus during horizontal ab/adduction. This keeps the arms in the same plane during the eccentric and concentric phases. The supraspinatus serves as the local stabilizer for the glenohumeral joint.

questions.question: Based on the principles of stability what exercise would be the most difficult to stabilize?
questions.explanation: Stability is affected by the base of support, location of the center of mass relative to the base of support, the height of the center of mass and the direction of the force relative to the direction of the base. The single-arm press affects several of these factors and since it is located on one side it forces more trunk stability to keep the body from "tipping". Interestingly, the physioball does not dramatically affect the stability when the loads are bilaterally symmetrical.

questions.question: To correct the movement error seen in the image, the client should be instructed to ____________.
questions.answer: take a broader step and push the back leg downward
wrong: lean backward and push the hips forward
lean forward and push the hips backward
pre-stretch the quadriceps and the glutes
questions.explanation: Both forward and reverse lunges are controlled by the back leg position. Taking a broad step forward or backward while pushing the back knee downward controls the pelvic location to prevent forward movement of the femur. When lunging, the tibia should be vertical not excessively forward. Excess dorsi flexion forces the knee to cross the toe, promoting undesirable translation of the tibia.

questions.question: Which of the following refers to the cumulative input to the nervous system that relays body and positional movement?
questions.explanation: Proprioceptors provide neural data to manage body movement and spatial awareness. The idea of proprioception suggests the body can move in a controlled manner with or without data provided from sight as the body "feels" the environment.

questions.question: What muscle is the prime mover for the lateral lunge performed in the picture?
questions.explanation: The prime movers for the lateral lunge are the hip abductors when a single anchor is used because the glute medius and tensor fascia latae (TFL) push outward in the frontal plane to return the center of mass to the starting point. The hip adductors do contribute to a lesser degree, but are actually stretched more than activated. Lateral kettlebell swings with changing anchors would be a better choice for addressing the hip adductors as the body "pulls" itself sideways.

questions.question: Clients should be instructed to retract the scapula during the seated row exercise in order to effectively train the:
questions.explanation: Concentric contraction of the rhomboids will cause the scapulae to retract, as seen during the correct performance of the bilateral row exercise. The rhomboids function to move the scapulae inward; providing the “squeezing sensation” felt in the upper back when activated properly.

questions.question: Aerobic metabolism in the muscle cell occurs within which organelle?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the organelles responsible for aerobic metabolism in muscle cells. The Krebs cycle occurs within the mitochondria; a process in which ATP is formed using oxygen and energy substrates from sugar, fat and protein. Aerobic training increases the number of mitochondria within muscle cells. This increase in mitochondrial density enables cells to produce more energy using oxygen extracted by the tissue.

questions.question: What anabolic hormone is released in higher concentrations in response to heavy resistance training?
questions.explanation: Whereas insulin-like growth factor is common to bodybuilding sets, testosterone release occurs with total-body loading at high intensities. Back squats and deadlifts are commonly sited as compound lifts that stimulate testosterone release following heavy sets. Insulin is another anabolic hormone but it functions to regulate metabolic functions, particularly sugar uptake by cells.

questions.question: Which pair of movements employ reciprocal inhibition in a training set aimed at improving strength and flexibility?
questions.answer: seated leg extension and hamstring stretch
questions.explanation: Since the quadriceps (agonist) pair with the hamstrings (antagonist) during knee extension, stretching the hamstrings following excitation of the quadriceps through resisted seated leg extension would create an ideal reciprocal match.

questions.question: Creatine phosphate, or the phosphagen system, would be the primary energy system for which of the following?
questions.explanation: The phosphagen system is a primary fuel source for high-intensity activities lasting 10-15 seconds, such as a 100-meter sprint, 3RM squat, or 60-yard shuttle. Circuit training and lactate threshold work would both rely more heavily on the glycolytic system while a 5K would require major contribution via the aerobic system.

questions.question: What type of contraction is performed by the abdominals and erector spinae muscle group during the performance of a push-up?
questions.explanation: An isometric contraction is performed by the abdominals and the erector spinae muscle group during the performance of a push-up, in accordance with the natural activity of local stabilizers. An isometric contraction creates an increase in muscle tension without changing any joint angles. In the case of a push-up, the abdominals and erector spinae help maintain the flat back position seen with proper technique.

questions.question: Increased blood flow to working muscles during a training session creates which of the following immediate effects during the workout?
questions.explanation: An influx of blood flow into working muscles gives the appearance of increased size via an acute “pump” action. Some supplements such as nitric oxide seek to increase this action as a vasodilator. All other adaptations or changes such as protein synthesis occur in the recovery period following the workout.

questions.question: What has the greatest impact on relative hormonal response during exercise?
questions.explanation: Intensity predicts the stress and metabolic demands of the body, consequentially triggering hormones to manage the internal condition. The higher the intensity of exercise, the more dramatic the hormone response to address the stress and energy demands. The more experienced the exerciser to the training environment the more adapted the hormone system will be to manage the stress. Chronological age does affect hormone response, but not to the extent of the prior.

questions.question: What causes the protein-sparing mechanism to shut off during exercise?
wrong: high amino acid concentration in the blood
creatine phosphate build-up in the cell
questions.explanation: Protein sparing occurs when adequate fuel is available and blood glucose levels can be maintained. When inadequate calories create low glycogen stores, with subsequent inadequate blood glucose, the body responds by sparing glycogen. Therefore, exercise with inadequate carbohydrates will cause protein catabolism to fuel ongoing physical activities.

questions.question: Besides differences in muscle mass and strength capabilities, why do females have lower cardiac outputs compared to males?
questions.explanation: Females have disadvantages for high VO2max values compared to men. They have smaller hearts relative to their size, which lowers oxygen delivery due to reduced cardiac output and less relative muscle mass which reduces capabilities for oxygen extraction. However, females do have capacities to improve with training similar to males, but not to the same peak levels.

questions.question: To optimize aerobic capacity the body increases oxygen delivery and extraction by adding:
questions.explanation: The body does not have the ability to add muscles and valves but can increase capillary density in a process called angiogenesis. Adding capillaries increases oxygen delivery to muscles and improves the diffusion of gases and nutrients. Additionally, the body adds mitochondria to process the energy to improve aerobic fitness.

questions.question: Floor to overhead reaching with a medicine ball would challenge _____________ to the greatest degree?
questions.explanation: Floor to overhead reaching would enhance spinal extension resistance and improve thoracic spine range of motion due to the movement pattern employed. The incorporation of the medicine ball in the reach enhances kinetic chain requirements.

questions.question: Which system of the body has the greatest adaptation rate when exposed to a new training stimulus?
questions.explanation: The nervous system has the fastest adaptation rate. As an extension of the brain, its motor “learning” capabilities are significant. Rapid increases in movement performance or strength are commonly seen among untrained individuals within 3-5 weeks. This early adaptation process to training is the body’s response to finding the most efficient way to manage a stress while exerting the least amount of energy. Improvements in the nervous system will promote increases in motor unit firing rates, recruitment, and synchronicity within the musculature being targeted.

questions.question: Delayed-onset muscle soreness generally appears:
questions.answer: 24-48 hours after a training session ends
wrong: 30-60 minutes after a training session starts
1-3 hours after a training session ends
questions.explanation: Delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) generally occurs 24-48 hours after a training session. DOMS is an inflammatory response that is believed to be caused by micro-tears in the muscle tissue caused by repeated contractions. Eccentric work is specifically known to elicit a DOMS response since the muscle tissue is forced to contract while it is lengthening, and can produce more force. A common misconception is that lactic acid causes DOMS; however, it should be recalled that lactic acid is a byproduct of glycolysis and is used as fuel. Increasing blood flow and oxygen to sore tissues seems to help relieve symptoms.

questions.question: The liver and skeletal muscle are primary storage sites for which of the following?
questions.explanation: Glycogen is the storage form of carbohydrates in the body, which is metabolized to provide glucose for energy. Major storage sites include skeletal muscles (300-400g), with about 75% of storage, and the liver (75-100g) containing the remaining 25%.

questions.question: Which of the following is a postural muscle characterized by a relatively greater slow-twitch fiber density?
questions.explanation: The soleus is a local postural stabilizer of the ankle. Due to its role in tibial position management it does not cross the knee as a mover. The high slow-twitch fiber density helps it maintain the tibia angle for potentially long periods of time during upright posture. To isolate the soleus one should perform a seated calf raise. The trapezius, gluteus medius, and obliques function as global movers and global stabilizers during activity.

questions.question: Which of the following recommendations would directly reduce the risk for heart disease?
questions.explanation: Negative stress is referred to as distress. Reducing chronic stress has been shown to have an immediate impact on reducing the risk for heart disease. Chronic stress elevates blood pressure and blood lipids; increasing the risk for vascular damage. This in turn elevates the risk for a cardiac event such as a heart attack.

questions.question: Advancing age and disease reduce vessel responsiveness called ______________ which increases risk for cardiovascular disease and stroke.
questions.explanation: Vascular compliance is defined as the ability of blood vessels to expand and contract, often measured as elasticity. Less compliant blood vessels no longer retain their ability to expand during hypertensive situations; causing further increases in blood pressure and vascular wall damage. Adaptations to aerobic exercise include increases in the diameter of blood vessels as well as improved compliance. These adaptations reduce the workload of the heart by decreasing peripheral resistance.

questions.question: Muscle fascia would be the target tissue for which of the following activities?
questions.explanation: Foam rolling is used to help reduce tension and multi-directional restriction within muscle fascia. When fascia is deformed due to imbalances or postural issues it reduces mobility and can create pain. Foam rolling can be implemented at any point in a training session as it does not impact strength or power like static stretches.

questions.question: What affect does routine, moderate-intensity aerobic training have on one's blood lipid profile?
wrong: Aerobic exercise does not affect circulating cholesterol
questions.explanation: An increase in HDL cholesterol can be seen with weekly aerobic training. High-density lipoproteins (HDL) are produced by the liver and serve a protective role in preventing arterial plaque formation, which can be increased with the presence of high LDL. An increase in HDL will facilitate increased transport of LDL cholesterol out of circulation. Specifically, HDL will carry LDL back to the liver (where it is formed); thwarting its immediate contribution to the progression of vascular disease.

questions.question: Increased midsection girth with age is a concern because:
questions.answer: android obesity is linked with heart disease
wrong: it indicates a low resting metabolism
gynoid obesity is linked with certain cancers
the obesity gene is triggered during middle age
questions.explanation: Android obesity is a term used to describe fat storage within the midsection of the human body, representing both subcutaneous and visceral fat. It is a major health concern based on its known relationship with heart disease. Android obesity is associated with an “apple-shape physique” and is dangerous because visceral fat cells release cytokines (chemicals) which promote systemic inflammation.

questions.question: What adaptation to aerobic exercise reduces heart rates at rest and during sub-maximal exercise?
questions.explanation: Increased stroke volume is an adaptation of the heart associated with appropriately-prescribed aerobic training. Aerobic training is known to increase the left ventricle’s contractile strength which increases the volume of blood expelled per contraction (stroke volume).

questions.question: What negative affect does trans fat have on blood lipid profiles?
questions.explanation: Trans fatty acids in the diet can lower HDL cholesterol and raise LDL cholesterol in the blood. The man-made fat is common in processed foods. Trans fat is used to adjust the textures of baked, fried and fast food products and should be avoided. The World Health Organization recommends it make up less than 1% of the diet.

questions.question: If your client presents with lower back pain, which assessment may be contraindicated during an initial assessment, especially if the cause of the pain is yet unknown?
questions.explanation: The trunk extension test may be contraindicated for a client with lower back pain of an unknown cause as the test requires hyperextension of the back. If there is a disc impingement issue this could easily be aggravated by the movement required.

questions.question: What positive change can be expected from an aerobic exercise intervention for a hypertensive client lasting several months?
questions.answer: a reduction of blood pressure by 10 points
wrong: a reduction of blood pressure by 15 points
a reduction of blood pressure by 20 points
a reduction of blood pressure by 25 points
questions.explanation: With a properly-executed exercise program, 3 months of training would plausibly reduce blood pressure by 8-11 mmHg. It is believed that these changes are brought about by alterations in the sympathetic nervous system that reduce peripheral resistance and increases renal secretion of sodium. Basically, an individual’s arteries are able to expand slightly, decreasing pressure immediately, and the kidneys are able to reduce salt content in the blood to further reduce pressure.

questions.question: What affect does chronic high blood insulin (hyperinsulinemia) have on body composition?
questions.explanation: A problem with consuming sugar and processed carbohydrates is the high release of insulin. Hyperinsulinemia weakens the effectiveness of the hormone, increasing the risk for diabetes, and also inhibits lipolysis increasing fat storage patterns. Chronic hyperinsulinemia is associated with metabolic disease and premature death from heart disease.

questions.question: Which of the following exercises could be difficult to perform for a client with tightness in their latissimus dorsi and triceps?
questions.explanation: The forward elbow position to maintain a safe rack for the bar during a front squat requires adequate mobility through shoulder and elbow flexion. Tightness in the shoulder and elbow extensors, namely the lats and triceps, can limit one’s ability to maintain the proper positioning throughout the movement.

questions.question: How is sarcopenia prevented with advancing age?
wrong: proper diet with calcium and vitamin D
questions.explanation: Strength, power, and free-weight training is optimal for promoting healthy aging. As the body ages muscle mass will decline without adequate training. This is accelerated with advancing age so those over 65 should focus on strength training.

questions.question: What affect does vascular peripheral resistance have on physical health?
questions.explanation: Decreased vascular compliance, plaque accumulation and reduced metabolic efficiency promote increased resistance in the vessels - which increases blood pressure. Since diastolic blood pressure is assessed during the relaxation phase of the heart it indicates the level of peripheral resistance.

questions.question: What type of performance training is most associated with independence for older adults?
questions.explanation: While each of the answers represent performance-related components of fitness, power is most associated with function and tasks of daily living among older adults. Balance training is recommended but improvements in movement economy occur with strength and power training.

questions.question: Turbulent blood flow associated with hypertension often leads to _________________ from vascular damage.
questions.explanation: When arteries narrow they increase the rate of blood flow much like placing a thumb on the end of a hose. This increase causes microtears in the vessel which start the cascade of events leading to coronary artery disease.

questions.question: Which of the following identifies the correct body fat classification for morbid obesity?
questions.explanation: Body fat levels above 30% for males and 40% for females are classified as morbid obesity and require a medical referral. Stage 1 Obesity can be classified using a BMI score of 30 or by a body fat percentage of 25% for males and 32% for females - which do not warrant medical referral. Female threshold values are higher due to the sex characteristics that promote and require higher fat storage.

questions.question: When working with elderly clients, what type of exercise is important to incorporate into the program?
questions.explanation: Closed-chain balance training should be incorporated into a comprehensive program for elderly clients due to their elevated risk for falling and related accidents. Closed-chain training, when appropriately applied, will greatly improve the nervous system’s ability to manage various forces, both in a static and dynamic nature. Programming dynamic balance will also help improve confidence, the client’s overall quality of life and performance during day-to-day activities.

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT be considered part of a pre-activity screening protocol?
wrong: Asking a client about low back pain
questions.explanation: VO2, or aerobic capacity, is assessed during exercise testing which requires appropriate clearance. Pre-exercise screening is used to reduce client risks and pave the way for personalized program development. Primary risk examples can include health status assessment of smoking, obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, asthma, peripheral edema, chronic low back pain, orthopedic injuries, sickle cell anemia, and autoimmune disease. Compounding risk examples can include older age, high fat/sugar diet, unmanaged stress, excessive alcohol intake, poor sleeping habits, low fiber intake, and musculoskeletal imbalances.

questions.question: Why do some assessments of anaerobic fitness, such as the abdominal curl-up and 5-min step test, require the use of a metronome?
questions.answer: to maintain a constant pace of movement
wrong: to ensure the client has rest intervals between repetitions
questions.explanation: The maintenance of a constant pattern of movement without rest is required in specific fitness assessments such as the abdominal curl-up and step tests to optimize movement control. Consistent movement timing reduces the risk of poor pacing and injury as well as prevents the use of momentum to aid in additional repetitions.

questions.question: Which exercise requires the greatest activation of the posterior deltoids?
questions.explanation: The reverse fly loads horizontal abduction, and the posterior deltoids are primary horizontal abductors. Chest flys do not activate the posterior deltoid, while upper back exercises do see contribution from the posterior deltoids depending on the pulling angle or grip used, but not to the extent seen during a reverse fly with extended elbows.

questions.question: Your client indicates a previous surgery on her left knee when completing the Health Status Questionnaire. What is the appropriate course of action for the trainer?
questions.answer: ask follow-up questions concerning the injury and any rehabilitation she completed
wrong: start the client using corrective exercise and eccentric activities
send the client to their doctor for a medical referral prior to performing any measurements
perform only upper-body resistance training and non-impact aerobic exercise
questions.explanation: The appropriate response for the trainer would be to ask follow-up questions concerning the injury, surgery and rehabilitation. Answers to the follow-up questions are essential when planning the most appropriate course of action. The severity of the injury as well as the comprehensiveness of any rehab will determine the starting point for exercise prescription. However, further testing specific to the left knee joint (e.g., flexibility and stability testing) would be appropriate in creating the most effective program.

questions.question: Identify the correct order of events when initiating activity for a new client.
questions.answer: informed consent, health status questionnaire, screening, exercise testing
wrong: exercise testing, informed consent, screening, health status questionnaire
health status questionnaire, exercise testing, screening, informed consent
screening, health status questionnaire, informed consent, exercise testing
questions.explanation: The correct order of events when working with a new client is to first implement the informed consent process, followed by the health status questionnaire (HSQ). The trainer can then perform any resting assessments and screen the client. Once cleared for exercise, the client can perform physical activity tests. The informed consent should always be first, as it informs and requests voluntary permission for health screening, fitness evaluations and exercise participation. This document will notify the participant of the inherent risks and facts about the subsequent testing/exercise. The HSQ is part of the screening process as it is used to help the trainer identify potential limitations and risks to activity participation.

questions.question: Your new, 54 year-old male client is 250 lbs. and has 25% body fat. What should you recommend to him regarding his initial participation in aerobic activity to lose weight?
questions.answer: To reduce the risk for injury, start with low-impact activities using a steady-state heart rate to acclimate him to exercise
wrong: He should primarily use high-intensity interval training for 60 minutes
He needs to see a physician for clearance and recommendations
Incorporate repeat anaerobic threshold circuits to maximize caloric expenditure
questions.explanation: With individuals that have a high level of body fat and are new to exercise, it is prudent to perform any aerobic work using low-impact activities. High-impact activities such as running increase the risk for overuse and orthopedic injuries among new exercisers. Likewise, high-intensity programs are not well-tolerated and are often contraindicated for obese individuals.

questions.question: Which assessment of aerobic fitness is most commonly used for a 14 year-old boy?
questions.explanation: The most appropriate assessment of aerobic fitness for a 14 year-old boy would be the one mile run test. Children can perform short bouts (10 minutes) of intense aerobic exercise on an intermittent basis. Care should be provided to ensure conditions account for thermoregulation, as youths have a greater tendency to overheat. When using any running tests the participant should practice the distance to establish an appropriate pace.

questions.question: Which of the following measures indicates the onset of hypertension?
wrong: All of the above are negative indicators
questions.explanation: Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is indicated with systolic blood pressure ≥130 mmHg and/or diastolic blood pressure ≥80 mmHg. Elevation in one value or the other is an indication of hypertension, it does not have to be both values.

questions.question: Your client just had knee surgery but wants to train while doing his rehabilitation. What is the correct course of action?
questions.answer: Get medical clearance and recommendations from his physician
wrong: Get the activities from their physical therapist to incorporate into the training
Just perform cardiovascular exercise with the lower limbs and resistance train the upper body
Perform the exercise in a pool to prevent weight bearing
questions.explanation: Any individual actively receiving care for a current injury from a medical professional should get clearance prior to engaging concurrent physiological systems. This clearance provides the "do's and don'ts", reducing both injury risk and trainer liability.

questions.question: Which of the following measurements would require your male client to obtain a medical clearance before participating in an exercise program due to cardiovascular disease risk?
questions.explanation: A client would require medical referral due to a resting heart rate greater than 100 beats per minute, as this indicates tachycardia. High resting heart rates increase the risk of arrhythmias. The resting heart rate should be assessed upon waking before any adrenal hormones impact the value; however, it is common for the personal trainer to measure it during a screening assessment. Therefore, clients should be instructed to sit or lie motionless for 2-5 minutes before the trainer assesses their radial pulse to attain a more accurate value. Follow-up assessments are warranted when measures above 90 beats per minute are obtained - as it could be test anxiety. Similarly, a client with a resting systolic blood pressure 160 or greater, or diastolic blood pressure 100 or greater would require medical referral as well.

questions.question: Which is a vital component of maintaining a professional certification credential?
questions.answer: All of the above are correct
wrong: complying with the profession's code of ethics
questions.explanation: Continuing education is a vital component of maintaining a professional credential and is mandated by the governing body to maintain certification status. Ethically, trainers should focus on continued education in their deficient areas and only represent the areas of knowledge for which they are competent. Additional professional requirements include maintaining CPR certification and complying with the code of ethics and professional practice.

questions.question: What causes blood pressure to rise the most with exercise and is considered unsafe for hypertensive clients?
questions.explanation: Clients with hypertension should avoid performing heavy resistance training sets as well as holding their breath. This is due to the increased stress placed upon the heart and vascular system during high-intensity resistance training. Resistance training for individuals with high blood pressure should not exceed intensities associated with 12-15 repetitions, nor include heavy compressive exercises such as the leg press.

questions.question: Professional standards and guidelines are dictated by all of the following, except:
questions.explanation: Fitness professionals may gain a wealth of practical experience as they work in the field, but personal interests and opinions cannot replace regulated standards and guidelines for the profession. Governmental entities (some involved in research), consumer expectations and safety, and employer requirements are all valid reference points for developing standards within the fitness field by reputable organizations and stakeholders.

questions.question: Which agonist-antagonist group is recommended to have a strength balance ratio of 1:1?
questions.explanation: The trunk flexors and extensors should have a strength balance ratio of 1:1 for core health and stability. The ankle plantar-flexors and dorsi-flexors should have a balance of 3:1 – while the knee and shoulder flexor-extensor relationships are recommended to be at 2:3.

questions.question: It is recommended that client files be maintained for at least 3 years upon their completion date due to:
questions.answer: Statutes of liability limitations can carry out that long
wrong: The likelihood they return to training
Reference for training others of similar need
In the case the documents are needed by law
questions.explanation: Statutes vary from state-to-state, but in general, three years is the minimum safe duration of time. Electronic files and copies are considered appropriate forms to avoid paper work.

questions.question: Bioelectrical impedance assessment results are impacted by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Various guidelines should be applied for a bioelectrical impedance test for body composition as many factors can impact the results. These can include eating/drink within 4 hours of the test, exercising within 12 hours of the test, consuming alcohol within 24 hours of the test, failing to use the restroom before the test to void the bowel/bladder if needed, failing to avoid assessment during menstruation, and using any diuretic medications as this will also impact hydration status.

questions.question: Which document is a useful tool in reducing liability due to the understanding of risk?
questions.explanation: The informed consent provides the client with details of the possible risks and expectations of exercise participation so they can make an informed decision to participate in the activities. While a signed informed consent protects the personal trainer against claims that the individual was not informed of the protocols and associated risks of training, it does not protect against negligence. The informed consent should always be the first document completed in the screening process.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key recommendation when creating an exercise prescription for an obese client?
questions.answer: select low-impact activities during aerobic exercise to minimize the risk of injury
wrong: maintain a caloric intake of 800-1,200 kcals per day
minimize weight lifting initially and perform more aerobic exercise
program to ensure that at least 250 kcals are burned per training session
questions.explanation: Exercise prescription for an obese client should include low-impact activities during aerobic exercise to minimize the risk of injury and over-exertion common of new exercisers. High-impact exercises such as treadmill running can cause severe DOMS or overuse injuries (e.g., shin splints). Likewise, obese individuals often prefer slower speeds and higher resistance over faster activities - such as changes in grade over speed.

questions.question: Your client hurts her knee while performing a step-up. She is in noticeable pain but does not want to stop the session. What is the correct course of action?
questions.answer: Recommend she have a medical professional analyze the injury and document the incident
wrong: Provide her with an anti-inflammatory if available and switch to upper-body exercises
Evaluate the injury and attempt to diagnose the problem before making a decision
Stretch the muscles surrounding the knee and lighten the weight before continuing
questions.explanation: When any injury occurs during a training session it should be formally documented for trainer protection. This includes documenting any witnesses. A follow-up by a medical professional can quickly ascertain problems, resolutions or provide clearance. Further injury is the greatest risk of an existing injury.

questions.question: The label on a nutrition bar states that it contains 40 grams of carbohydrates and 10 grams of sugar. What percentage of the carbohydrates represent sugars?
questions.explanation: Dividing 10 grams (sugar) by 40 grams (total carbs) would equal 0.25, or 25%. Sugar is always listed on the food label as part of the total carbohydrates included as well as an independent nutritional factor.

questions.question: Adequate intake of __________ is vital for individuals with hypertension due to its relationship with sodium.
questions.explanation: Sodium and potassium function cooperatively through nutrient balance. While potassium should usually be consumed at levels that surpass sodium, many people overconsume sodium and under-consume potassium. Potassium is essential for proper nerve and muscle function due to its role in cellular fluid and solute regulation throughout the body.

questions.question: Which is a protein derived from cow’s milk?
questions.explanation: Both whey and casein are quality sources of supplemental protein derived from cow’s milk. Albumen is derived from egg protein while protease is a protein enzyme. Phytonutrients are primarily found in colorful fruits and vegetables, so they are not contained in milk.

questions.question: When Vitamin D, a fat-soluble vitamin, is ingested how many calories are absorbed per gram of vitamin D consumed?
questions.explanation: Micronutrients do not contain any calories. Vitamins are fat-soluble or water-soluble which defines the transport and storage mechanism, not the caloric density. This is true for all vitamins and minerals.

questions.question: Your client has heard about the potential for quick weight loss associated with low-carbohydrate diets. Which response is accurate regarding these types of diets?
questions.answer: lean mass and central nervous system efficiency will be negatively impacted by a low-carbohydrate diet
wrong: the low carbohydrate intake will help burn more fat during anaerobic training
metabolic rate is significantly increased in individuals who consume a low-carbohydrate diet
low carbohydrate intake will improve the body's ability to burn calories during aerobic training
questions.explanation: Low-carbohydrate diets negatively affect the body by reducing glycogen reserves and decreasing plasma glucose; which in turn stimulates the production of new glucose in the liver via a process known as gluconeogenesis. During gluconeogenesis, glucose is synthesized from metabolites and amino acids. During glycogen-sparing, the body releases adrenal hormones to break down lean mass and fat mass for fuel; which is an undesirable effect. Low-carbohydrate diets are intended to cut sugar and processed carbs but tend to also reduce quality sources. Initial weight loss from low-carbohydrate diets is majorly due to a loss in total body water as glycogen stores are reduced. The central nervous system needs adequate systemic carbohydrates to run efficiently. Overall, this can reduce total work capacity.

questions.question: What fat-soluble vitamin may create toxicity if supplemented in high doses?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A, a fat-soluble vitamin along with vitamins E, K and D, are stored in adipose tissue. Due to their metabolic properties, supplemental fat-soluble vitamins can potentially cause toxicity when over-consumed. A well-balanced diet will generally meet the daily intake demands of all fat-soluble vitamins; providing little need for additional supplementation.

questions.question: Dehydration contributes to a reduction in blood plasma volume. How will this impact training performance?
questions.answer: it will increase heart rate values during training
wrong: it will increase muscle blood flow during resistance training
questions.explanation: Decreased blood plasma volume as a result of dehydration will impact training by increasing heart rates. This is due to reduced blood volume and subsequent reductions in cardiac output. The physiological explanation for this is that most of the fluid lost in sweat is supplied by blood plasma. If rehydration is not established, there is an increased possibility for kidney and heart dysfunction as well as heat stroke during exhaustive exercise.

questions.question: Which of the following denotes low blood sodium?
questions.explanation: Hyponatremia is a term used to describe low blood sodium. This can be caused in severe cases where significant fluids replaced after high-volume training and dehydration do not contain any electrolytes such as sodium. Low blood electrolytes creates a dangerous situation which can cause neural and even cardiac muscle-related issues.

questions.question: Static stability would be challenged the most by which of the following exercises?
questions.explanation: Static stability indicates a stabilization challenge where joints are maintained in position and isometric contractions are employed. High static stability allows one to maintain proper form and posture even with forces which seek to disrupt joint positioning. Planks and bridges are major examples of core training exercises which challenge static stability. "

questions.question: ____________ content lowers the insulin response to a meal.
questions.explanation: Fiber decreases gastric emptying rate and slows the blood glucose absorption process, thereby reducing insulin effects. This positively manages glucose levels and lowers the negative impact of insulin on lipid metabolism.

questions.question: All of the following statements concerning intermittent fasting is correct, except:
questions.answer: It has been shown to create cyclical fat gain due to reduced metabolism
wrong: It has been shown to improve metabolic health
It can improve weight loss over time
All eating usually occurs over a period of 3-8 hours per day
questions.explanation: Intermittent fasting appears to promote weight loss and may improve gut and metabolic health; the evidence is particularly strong when the eating pattern reduces or eliminates nighttime feeding. It includes several patterns of fasting and non-fasting over defined periods - independent of caloric restriction. Often all eating occurs over a period of 3-8 hours per day, which inherently creates intermittent ‘fasting periods’ between.

questions.question: _________ is an amino acid which is purported to stimulate the growth hormone production.
questions.explanation: L-arginine is an amino acid that functions in the process of growth hormone management. Supplementation has not provided scientifically-significant ergogenic benefits to date. Glutamine is an anticorticoid agent protecting muscle protein while leucine and valine serve as two of the three branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs).

questions.question: Which of the following is true concerning circumference (girth) measurements when used as a body composition assessment?
questions.answer: they have limited accuracy for individuals with large amounts of muscle mass
wrong: they are ideal for individuals who are very thin
they do not account for subcutaneous fat storage
they are not designed to be used for obese individuals
questions.explanation: While girth/circumference measurements are very applicable with overweight individuals; they have limited accuracy for individuals with large amounts of muscle mass inclusive of athletes. Girth/circumference measurements do not take into account the volume of each respective tissue at each site of measurement. For lean individuals with larger quantities of muscle, a skinfold assessment would be more appropriate. For those who are very muscular but also maintain higher fat, hydrostatic weighing would be more useful, and is generally the most accurate outside of DEXA scans.

questions.question: Your client is a 17 year-old male trying to add muscle mass. Which of the following would be a healthy dietary recommendation to support his resistance training program that includes four days of exercise each week?
questions.answer: he should strive for a positive caloric balance of roughly 150-450 calories per day to ensure he meets his protein requirements
wrong: he should consume an additional 750-1,000 kcal a day above his calculated daily requirements
he should aim for a neutral caloric balance to convert his fat into muscle
he should consume 40-50% of his total daily calories from protein to ensure adequate muscle gains
questions.explanation: When a client is trying to add muscle mass, the healthy recommendation would be to consume a positive caloric balance of roughly 150-450 kcal per day. Combining resistance training with the additional caloric intake (and a focus on quality proteins) would help him achieve his goal. In some circumstances among those who are resistant to gaining weight, consuming 500-750 kcal above daily requirements may be needed.

questions.question: All of the following will increase testing accuracy for a skinfold assessment, except:
wrong: Identifying and marking the skinfold sites
Assessing the fold in 2 secs and waiting at least 15 seconds before re-assessing
questions.explanation: Adding more sites to a skinfold assessment does not increase accuracy when proper density equations are use. In fact, 7- and 12-site measures are associated with increased tester error due to the amount of technician involvement. Repeat 3- and 4-fold sites have an estimation error of about 4% (3.5%).

questions.question: Which of the following is the most effective way to reduce the appearance of cellulite?
questions.answer: Reduce total body fat via diet and exercise
wrong: Perform high-volume exercises which target the areas cellulite is most visible
questions.explanation: Reducing total body fat and weight will reduce the appearance of cellulite. Changing the macronutrient content of the diet will not have an impact if a negative caloric balance is not attained. CLA has not demonstrated any efficacy and spot reduction of cellulite (e.g., performing numerous crunches to reduce belly cellulite) is not effective as it will only improve the muscle tonicity in the area, but you cannot force localized fat use.

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning anorexia nervosa is correct?
questions.answer: It is associated with significant body image disorder
wrong: It is most common among males
It has the lowest rate of mortality among psychiatric disorders
All of the above are correct
questions.explanation: Anorexia nervosa is always associated with significant body image disorder, an intense fear of getting fat and a preoccupation with body weight. It is most common among females and has one of the highest rates of mortality among psychiatric conditions, often sadly due to suicide.

questions.question: After calculating your client's resting metabolic rate to be ~2,200 calories/day, she performs a 24-hour food recall which indicates approximately 2,500 calories were consumed. What can you determine from this information?
questions.answer: nothing until you calculate voluntary caloric expenditure
wrong: your client is in a negative caloric balance
your client's oxygen consumption is relatively high
your client is in a positive caloric balance
questions.explanation: Resting metabolic rate and voluntary caloric expenditure are both required to determine daily caloric need. Resting metabolism will generally constitute 60-70% of total metabolism (and therefore energy needs), while physical activity and the thermic effect of food make up the difference based on individual choices.

questions.question: What would cause a disproportionately-low abdominal skinfold measurement on a middle-age male client with a visibly large abdominal area?
questions.answer: excessive amounts of visceral fat storage
wrong: central hypertrophy of the rectus abdominis
questions.explanation: Excessive visceral fat storage would cause a high central girth, which may occur in conjunction with relatively low abdominal skinfold measures. Some individuals present with limited subcutaneous measures but high levels of abdominal visceral fat; which can only be indirectly assessed with a central girth measurement. Visceral fat is associated with cardiovascular and metabolic disease.

questions.question: Your female client wants to tone up. While she has enough fat stored throughout her body to justify becoming leaner, she is at a visibly healthy body weight. What is the best field assessment to measure body fat given her appearance?
questions.explanation: Skinfold measures would be the most appropriate body fat assessment for this individual given their relatively lean body type. Skinfold measurements should not be used with overweight or obese populations, as the procedure is psychologically-invasive and the measurements will be skewed by the high fat content. This is particularly applicable in the trunk as visceral fat storage cannot be determined by using skinfolds.

questions.question: How should you explain the need for regular aerobic activity within a weight loss program to your client who thinks they can simply alter their diet and perform resistance training twice a week to achieve the desired results?
wrong: aerobic exercise will help create a negative caloric balance
increasing VO2max improves anaerobic training by reducing necessary rest interval time
questions.explanation: All answers listed are adaptations to aerobic training that will aid in weight loss. While dietary modifications and resistance training twice a week will aid in weight reduction, the addition of aerobic training will greatly expedite results due to (1) additional caloric expenditure, (2) the additional use of fat as a fuel, and (3) the ability to increase the client’s work capacity during their resistance training sessions (due to improved recovery between sets) - which can eventually be progressed into more aggressive aerobic and anaerobic interval training. In general, high intensity exercise will be most effective for weight loss due to the caloric expenditure.

questions.question: You are working with a female client who does not want to "bulk up" from weight training. Which of the following set-rep schematics performed in three, 60-minute sessions per week would have the greatest desired adaptation effect?
questions.answer: 4 sets per exercise at 65% 1RM, 15 reps, 30-45 second rest intervals, total body emphasis
wrong: 3 sets per exercise at 80-90% 1RM, 4-8 reps, 75-90 second rest intervals, muscle group-specific exercise
5 sets per exercise using 70-80% 1RM, 10-12 reps, 60-75 second rest intervals, upper or lower body
2 sets per exercise using 30-60% 1RM, 8-10 reps, 2 minute rest intervals, ballistic jumps and throws
questions.explanation: Higher and very low repetition ranges reduce muscle architectural changes. Low reps at 1-3 works the nervous system while high reps over 15 work the metabolic system. 15 reps equal about 45 seconds of time-under-tension, with a 1:1 to 1:0.75 ratio. This type of training is specifically geared towards caloric expenditure with a side benefit of maintaining lean mass. However, due the elevated time-under-tension (>20 minutes), it may not be feasible for a novice client unaccustomed to resistance training.

questions.question: When reducing dietary fat intake, _________ should make up the majority of dietary lipids consumed?
questions.explanation: Monounsaturated fatty acids are cardiovascularly-benign. Common to the Mediterranean diet these fatty acids should represent between 15-20% of the fats in the diet. Saturated fat should be less than 10%, and polyunsaturated fats generally reach up to 15%. Trans fatty acids should be avoided.

questions.question: Which of the following muscle groups require hip flexion and external rotation of the hip to properly stretch?
questions.explanation: The piriformis is an external rotator of the hip. While normally a muscle needs the opposite movement direction to stretch - the piriformis uses external rotation with hip flexion as it traps and lengthens the muscle in this position.

questions.question: The risk of blood pooling after a high-intensity training session is minimized by ___________.
questions.explanation: An appropriate cool down will help minimize blood pooling after a high-intensity training session. A proper cool down should include full range of motion movements that mimic the exercises included in the resistance training bout to bring the body back to a pre-exercise state. The emphasis should be on fluid movements that are continuous enough to mobilize circulating blood in venous structures.

questions.question: The device used to measure specific joint angles during a flexibility assessment is referred to as a ____________.
questions.explanation: The goniometer is a device used to directly measure joint angles. Goniometric measurements help identify range of motion deficits which can potentially contribute to injury. While valid and reliable as a form of assessment, successful readings are determined by protocol strictness and technician expertise.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common caloric pitfall during cardiovascular training on aerobic machines?
wrong: Leaning one’s weight on the machine being used
Failing to comply with default RPMs
questions.explanation: Leaning into a Stairclimber or holding on to the treadmill reduces the caloric expenditure as a percentage of the body is being supported, thereby reducing the workload and intensity. This will also alter the accuracy of any machine calculation for caloric expenditure as it accounts for total body weight as part of the equation. Failing to comply with default RPMs will create the same discrepancy in caloric expenditure.

questions.question: Which back exercise employs the biceps to the greatest degree?
questions.explanation: Switching from a pronated grip to a supinated grip will allow for greater biceps activation when performing back exercises. The chin-up uses a supinated grip which still targets the back musculature, but also increases demands placed upon the elbow flexors.

questions.question: The _______________ are manipulated during proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation stretches to enhance the attainable movement range.
questions.explanation: Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) stretches create high tension at the tendons where Golgi tendon organs adjust to allow a slight increase in movement range following maximal activation of the musculature involved. These stretches present an increased risk of injury via overstrain - and should only be implemented by a skilled trainer on healthy, experienced individuals.

questions.question: Using _______ repetitions per set during a general fitness or preparation phase for a new, healthy client would be appropriate to develop baseline form and technique mastery.
questions.explanation: 10-15 repetitions per set during a general fitness/preparation training program allows for enough movement practice to gain technique mastery and also allows for appropriate starting intensities to be applied. The 6-10 repetitions range would be more appropriate for strength or hypertrophy with elevated loads, while 20-30 repetition sets would delve into anaerobic threshold training depending on how it was applied.

questions.question: Approximately how many calories does the body use per liter of consumed oxygen?
questions.explanation: Approximately 5 calories are utilized per liter of oxygen consumed. Oxygen consumption as a measure of caloric expenditure is simplified when using metabolic equivalents (METs). METs represent the amount of oxygen used by the body at a given time. For instance, rest = 1 MET; whereas walking = 2.8 METs. METS x .001 x BW (kg) x minutes performed x 5 kcal = Total calories burned

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important consideration when performing a heavy or complex exercise?
questions.explanation: No matter what exercise is performed form and technique must be properly adhered to prevent injury - particularly when using complex variations as the risk for injury may increase. Proper form and technique must be the baseline focus before any other considerations are addressed. Too much weight is a common programmatic error affecting performance regardless of age.

questions.question: When designing an exercise program for a healthy novice client, resistance training exercises should be performed using which of the following set-repetition schematics?
questions.explanation: The most appropriate sets and reps for a novice client, when they are being introduced to resistance training, would 2-3 sets of 10-15 repetitions. Higher-repetition schemes improve neuromuscular efficiency and allow the client to perfect the movement pattern through rehearsal. Lower intensities reduce the risk of acute musculoskeletal injuries as well as discomfort from excessive soreness associated with heavy eccentric contractions. Once acclimated, the client can be further challenged with loading or movement variations.

questions.question: What most affects the rate of detraining when switching from 4 days to two days of training per week?
questions.explanation: Increasing the training intensity (%) is necessary to minimize losses of strength and power when frequency is reduced. Exercise prescription should focus on the proper execution of intense and high-velocity activities. For less fit individuals, incorporating compound exercises would be preferred over isolated (machine) lifts.

questions.question: Resistance machines are optimally suited for ______________ training due to the ability to isolate a muscle and aid in stability.
questions.explanation: Machine-based resistance training promotes greater hypertrophic responses due to the reduced requirement of stabilizer muscle contribution; allowing more weight to be placed upon the prime mover. Removing stabilizers from the equation allows the targeted muscle to be further isolated, increasing total time-under-tension.

questions.question: Groups of exercises performed in sequence with limited rest is called _________________.
questions.explanation: A circuit training bout sequences multiple exercises in descending order of difficulty with only transitional rest periods. Popular circuit training prescription involves the use of an exercise for each of the major muscle groups, but many variations exist.

questions.question: Performing cardiovascular exercise for a duration of time with a consistent same heart rate is termed:
questions.explanation: When a constant heart rate is achieved during aerobic training, it is classified as steady state training. Steady state is defined by a change in heart rate by less than 5 beats per minute.

questions.question: If a client has shoulder impingement they will likely be unable to perform:
questions.explanation: Shoulder impingement occurs when the supraspinatus tendon and shoulder bursa get pinched at the acromion. This occurs when the arm is elevated, particularly with internal rotation. Avoiding overhead lifts such as the military press and internal rotation with shoulder flexion can alleviate pain. Pressing exercises and upright rows are common agitators.

questions.question: What indicates the amount of oxygen used by working tissue?
questions.explanation: Whereas stroke volume and heart rate represent cardiac output, the (a-v)O2 difference is the difference in oxygen concentration between arterial blood and venous blood; representing extraction. This is also referred to as oxygen uptake. During exercise, the efficiency of the body’s oxygen extraction system is based on the number of capillaries and available oxygen to working tissue to be used by mitochondria. Improvements in the (a-v)O2 difference occur following aerobic training as the increased oxygen demands of the exercising muscles promotes increased capillary and mitochondrial density.

questions.question: What training intensity range is most effective for natural bodybuilding?
questions.explanation: 70-85% is the ideal training intensity for lifts used to promote protein synthesis. The goal of adding lean mass requires significant muscle recruitment and anabolic hormone release, which is best achieved with sets of 8-12 repetitions. Clients looking to add lean mass and gain significant strength may benefit from 6-10 repetitions.

questions.question: Why does using only the heart rate max formula by itself create potential problems for training specificity when programming aerobic training zones (220-age)?
wrong: It assumes all people of equal age are the same
It has at least a 10-12 beat standard estimation error
It does not account for variations in fitness level among individuals
questions.explanation: 10-12 beats/min represents the standard deviation for the Heart Rate Max (HRM) formula, which is an important consideration for setting intensities during aerobic training. About 68% of people will have a maximal heart rate within 10-12 beats of the estimated number, 95% will have a maximal heart rate within 20-24 beats, and 99.7% of people should have a maximal heart rate within 30-36 beats. While the HRM formula is commonly used, it does assume everyone of a certain age has the same maximal heart rate, which is a dependent variable and therefore not actually true. The formula also includes no variables which take into account variations in fitness level among people of the same age.

questions.question: When a trainer accurately identifies the best exercise to address a desired affect they are using the principle of ___________.
questions.explanation: Specific adaptations occur based on the specificity of the demand. The principle of specificity suggests all the parameters of the activity must be synchronized to create the desired affect from the training. For example, a superset that emphasizes the same muscle group for the desired goal of muscle hypertrophy is employing the principle of specificity.

questions.question: Diverse training speeds and terrains used for aerobic conditioning performed outside is a type of interval training termed _______.
questions.explanation: The idea of combining jogging with sprinting over hills, plateaus, and declines describes Fartlek training. It provides diversity to the intensity across a broad range of stressors. This is common to keep distance exercise both engaging and relatively challenging.

questions.question: Based on the neuro-metabolic system continuum, which training activities would be performed last when cross training?
questions.explanation: Power and strength rely heavily on the phosphagen system, hypertrophy and anaerobic endurance rely on glycogen, whereas aerobic training uses the most efficient system - but at the cost of force production. Aerobic endurance training is performed last in the main bout of exercise. Dynamic flexibility is used during warm-up and cool down periods, followed by foam rolling and static stretching.

questions.question: Approximately _______ hours of recovery should be allowed between intense or high-volume leg training sessions.
questions.explanation: The recovery period is defined as the duration of time between exercise sessions. It allows for energy system replenishment and adaptations to occur in response to the training stimulus. Inadequate recovery increases the risk for injury, illness, and overtraining while excess recovery limits fitness improvements. The minimum recovery period between intense leg training sessions is 48 hours (day off in between). This could vary based on other training and life variables as well as the condition of the client.

questions.question: Why do machines allow for more weight to be lifted than during free weight exercises?
questions.answer: The body experiences significantly reduced stabilization
wrong: The actual weight is not lifted by the body
questions.explanation: Machine-based resistance training reduces the requirement of the stabilizer muscles; allowing more weight to be placed upon the prime mover. Removing the stabilizers from the equation allows the targeted muscle to be further isolated, increasing total time-under-tension explaining why machines are used for hypertrophy training.

questions.question: Which of the following allows muscles to pull upon bones for movement?
questions.explanation: Tendons connect muscle to bone while ligaments primarily serve to attach bones to bones within a joint. Fascia is a type of connective tissue which encompasses skeletal muscle, providing shape.

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning negative sets is correct?
questions.answer: They require a spot-assisted concentric phase
wrong: They are useful for maximizing hypertrophy
The eccentric phase is performed using 80-90% of maximum
None of the above are correct
questions.explanation: Negative sets were popularized by early strength athletes to maximize force output and overcome training plateaus by increasing stability demands at high weight. The eccentric phase is performed using 110-130% of maximum, followed by a spot-assisted concentric phase. Using negative sets may not be appropriate for all clients due to the benefit-to-risk ratio and the effects on blood pressure; but it can be valuable during functional body-weight lifts such as Nordic hamstrings, push-ups, and pull-ups.

questions.question: What common disease can benefit from programming resistance and aerobic exercise?
questions.explanation: Both aerobic and anaerobic exercise can reduce the risk of developing disease. Strength training is particularly useful to excite both muscle and bone cells. Diabetes and obesity-related inflammation require both types of exercise, as the risk for heart disease is increased and aerobic training adds benefits to hormone management.

questions.question: You are training a healthy 35 year-old male who states his main goal is to add 20 lbs. of muscle. He is hoping to attain these gains by working with you twice a week for 60 minutes over the next 3 months. How should you advise this client?
questions.answer: the total training duration and volume per week is too low for the desired outcome
wrong: the goal can be met with proper muscle group emphasis, but there is no time for cardiovascular training
one-hour is not long enough for effective hypertrophy training
two sessions per week for a hypertrophy training goal would require the use of circuits for adequate volume
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy-specific training requires a volume unattainable with only two resistance training sessions per week. Likewise adding 20 lbs. of muscle in 3 months is unlikely unless the individual is severely malnourished or on anabolic steroids. In this example, the total training duration and volume per week is too low for the desired outcome, and this should be clarified with the client.

questions.question: Which of the following will help a client improve their aerobic capacity at the fastest relative rate?
questions.explanation: Interval training has been shown to yield the fastest positive aerobic adaptation responses. Steady-state and long, slow distance training yield benefits and can contribute to significant caloric expenditure, but the adaptation rate is not the same as the intensity is lower. Paired supersets can increase training volume and provide limited aerobic benefits, but the focus is still more on anaerobic adaptations.
