questions.question: Which of the following is being stretched in the image shown?
questions.explanation: The gastrocnemius (calf) is being stretched in the image presented. Flexing the hip and dorsiflexing the ankle places stretching tension upon the plantar flexors. Note that the knee must be kept fully extended to stretch the gastrocnemius specifically, flexion of the knee involves more of the soleus. Also, keeping the alternate leg flat on the ground maintains a more aggressive stretch response, this is especially true if performing a hamstring stretch from a similar position.

questions.question: What is the appropriate rest interval between 2 sets of power cleans using 85% 1RM?
questions.explanation: The clean exercise by definition is a power-based exercise. Power training in general tends to warrant longer recovery. When performed using heavy weight the recovery demands of the phosphagen system extend to about 2-3 minutes or rest between repeatable sets. Generally, aerobic training will vary from 1:1 to 1:3 work-to-rest ratios while anaerobic training will vary from 1:3 to 1:12 work to rest ratios.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding pyramid training is accurate?
questions.answer: The system is used for strength development as it provides progressive overload and neural enhancements
wrong: The system uses high repetitions and short rest intervals
The system is used for power development when integrated with plyometrics
The system is optimal for beginners seeking hypertrophy through motor rehearsal
questions.explanation: Pyramid training is ideal for strength development due to the progressive overload and neural enhancements it provides. This is seen applied with progressive increases in weight, and corresponding reductions in the repetitions performed each set. The repetitions generally progress downward into neuromuscular schemes of 3-5 repetitions (Example: 10, 8, 6, 5)

questions.question: What factor determines the adaptations that enhance muscular strength the most?
wrong: the number of days performed per week
questions.explanation: Intensity is the greatest predictor of strength adaptations. Since muscular strength is based on stability and force production - the more intense the activity the more the neuromuscular system is challenged. Since adaptations are relative, the gender of the exerciser does not matter; while men have a greater capacity for relative and absolute upper body strength, lower body relative strength gains are similar between elite males and females.

questions.question: What training system is used to improve both strength and power simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Contrast sets exploit the neuromuscular crossover exhibited between strength and power training by maximizing fast-twitch fiber recruitment within the working muscle groups. Circuit training is most appropriate to decrease one's risk for metabolic syndrome and should not emphasize strength or high-intensity power output. Combination exercises are when two exercises are fused to form one, like a front squat to press - their emphasis is connectivity, function, and power. Compound sets are similar to combination sets, except that there is a pause between the movements, e.g. a Clean and Jerk. Compound sets generally have a greater emphasis on connectivity and power.

questions.question: Which of the following training systems would provide the best adaptation for muscle hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Supersets are a useful tool for hypertrophy training as they allow for increased training volume and maximal fatigue/intensity placed upon the muscles of focus Negative sets are useful for strength and overcoming a lift-specific plateau. Lactate tolerance training is great for overall conditioning, but the loading and muscle-specific volume are not optimal for hypertrophy-specific adaptations.

questions.question: Which of the following is a combination exercise?
questions.explanation: Combination exercises by definition are single movements which combine two exercises into one – such as a lunge with rotation or front squat to overhead press. Both activities end at the same time combining force production. They are only feasible if the transition point between the two actions allows for continual movement from one to the other. Compound sets are similar, such as a clean and jerk, but there is a point between the two lifts where the action is stopped - and form is reset to begin the second action based on the desirable location of the load.

questions.question: Which selection would be the best choice for improving upper body power?
wrong: lunge to press with a dumbbell
alternating dumbbell press superset with dips
questions.explanation: If upper body power is the training goal, the most applicable exercise would be the incline medicine ball chest pass due to the velocity of the movement. If lower body power was the goal a good example could be box jumps for the same reason. Power is calculated by force x distance divided by time. Using 30-60% 1RM allows for peak power production as the lower weight allows for higher speeds of movement. For older clients, use relatively light resistance and focus on concentric-only throws to reduce the risk for injury.

questions.question: What instruction should be made to correct the performance of the lat pull-down exercise in the picture?
questions.answer: have the client pull the bar in front of their head instead of behind
wrong: instruct the client to flex the trunk at the terminal point to maximize the ROM
lean forward during the pulling phase
reduce the width of the hand position
questions.explanation: For the situation presented in the picture, the appropriate course of action would be to instruct the client to pull the bar to their chest instead of behind the head. Performing the lat pull-down behind the neck is contraindicated and increases the risk for a cervical spine injury as well as soft-tissue injuries within the shoulder joints. Furthermore, this form will actually reduce contribution from the latissimus dorsi muscles when compared to pulling in front of the head.

questions.question: Lack of heat loss via ____________ while training in a hot and humid environment presents the greatest risk for heat-related illnesses.
questions.explanation: Heat loss via sweat evaporation, which is derived from blood plasma, is limited in humid environments. Hot environments make it difficult for the body to lose heat via radiation, convection and conduction as well. So, hot and humid environments can create a much greater risk for heat-related illnesses and severe dehydration.

questions.question: What activity is used as a functional exercise for improving shoulder horizontal abduction?
questions.answer: Modified pull-up (feet on ground supine pull-up)
wrong: Modified push-up (hands on a bench decline push-up)
questions.explanation: The modified pull-up requires shoulder horizontal abduction. Rowing exercises where the humerus is abducted occur in the transverse plane and are useful for muscle balance and aid in performing body-weighted functional tasks.

questions.question: What is the best exercise to strengthen the lats in the frontal plane?
questions.explanation: The pull-up exercise is the best selection for training the lats in the frontal plane. The lat pull-down is performed in the frontal plane as well - and is therefore a valid choice for increasing strength in the humeral adductors - but pulling behind the head reduces lat activation and is contraindicated due to risk for injury.

questions.question: What exercise most effectively trains the obliques?
questions.explanation: The obliques function to rotate and laterally flex the spine. Therefore, the medicine ball rotational pass would be the best choice to train the muscular system. Back extension works the erector spinae while hanging leg raises focus on the rectus abdominis.

questions.question: A neutral lumbar spine is desirable during overhead exercises, requiring it to maintain a ________.
questions.explanation: The lumbar spine's natural position is a lordotic curve. It is undesirable to increase the curvature or flatten the back as it places undue stress on the spine. A common error with overhead lifts is to lean backwards to employ assistance from the chest causing lumbar lordosis; an undesirable exaggerated lumbar curve.

questions.question: During pull-ups the correct position at the bottom requires the:
questions.explanation: The arms are fully flexed at the top of the movement and fully extended at the bottom of the movement. Flexing the arms before the complete descent is common to individuals who are weak as they attempt to rely on the biceps to assist the movement. If spotting is required to maintain proper form, make sure to provide assistance at the client’s waist or lower ribcage.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an AED during emergency situations?
questions.answer: To defibrillate a heart which has flatlined
wrong: To minimize the impact of heat stroke
To stabilize a joint after a ligament tear
questions.explanation: An automatic external defibrillator (AED) is used on an individual in asystole. It does not have an impact on blood sugar, body temperature or joint stability. It is used to shock the heart back online.

questions.question: Which of the following exercises involves the deltoid as a prime mover?
questions.explanation: The upright row requires the humerus to be abducted to lift the load – which relies upon the anterior and medial deltoid. Shrugs primarily activate the trapezius while rows and pull-downs focus on latissimus dorsi and rhomboid activation.

questions.question: Your new client is a 66 year-old female who is concerned about her bone health. What should be included in her program to address her concerns?
questions.answer: resistance and power training using all major muscle groups
wrong: aerobic activities to minimize the risk of fractures and related injuries
flexibility training to balance out connective tissues
circuits of non-impact aerobic exercises mixed with passive stretching
questions.explanation: Aging female clients are at risk for developing osteopenia/osteoporosis and sarcopenia; therefore, exercise bouts should include both resistance and power training using all major muscle groups. Weight-bearing activities have been shown to help improve bone health and reverse the age-related decline in bone density.

questions.question: Your client is performing a seated leg extension on a machine and requires a spot to complete the final repetition. Where should you provide assistance?
questions.answer: at the pad or lever arm being moved
wrong: at the cable of the machine
questions.explanation: In order to provide assistance when a client is performing the seated leg extension, the personal trainer should apply force to the pad or lever arm. Do not provide direct assistance to the client’s body as complete failure could result in injury to all involved.

questions.question: When constructing a program for your client, what intensity should you select for the leg press if you want them to complete eight (8) repetitions to volitional fatigue?
questions.explanation: Performing eight repetitions to volitional fatigue will correlate with 80% of the 1RM. Choosing the proper intensity is essential when trying to optimize the effectiveness and efficiency of a training program towards desired adaptations. The relative intensity along with rest interval and number of repetitions determine the outcome; whether it is endurance, muscle hypertrophy, strength, power or function.

questions.question: What is the proper cue if a client presents with tibial translation (forward knee) during a back squat?
questions.answer: Push your hips back as you flex your knees
questions.explanation: Tibial translation occurs when the knees cross the toes and can be detrimental to the knee joints during various loaded conditions - particularly back squats. If this occurs during a back squat the trainer can cue the client to push their hips back simultaneously as they flex the knees to reduce the excessive dorsi flexion. Additional flexion at the trunk or knees would be improper cues during this exercise and elevating the chest will not impact hip position as needed.

questions.question: Which of the following exercises represents a functional task for the knee joint?
questions.explanation: The box step-up replicates a functional task of daily living. The other choices all use machines, or in the case of a straight-leg deadlift is contraindicated due to the locked leg position. Employing movements that replicate the use of muscle in real world applications provide for improvements in functional tasks such as climbing stairs.

questions.question: Which of the following would need to be fixed during a cable fly exercise?
questions.answer: Flexing at the spine during the concentric phase
wrong: Maintaining an erect spine during the eccentric phase
questions.explanation: A split stance can be used to enhance stability as long as the hips or trunk do not move. Slightly bending at the elbows is appropriate as long as the mild angle is maintained. Flexing or extending the hips and trunk to assist the movement via momentum indicates the load may need to be lowered or technique is not mastered. The spine should not flex.

questions.question: What is the ideal hand position when spotting the lat pull-down exercise?
questions.explanation: When spotting the lat pull-down exercise, the trainer should stand behind the client and place both hands on the bar using a pronated grip (palms down) to provide assistance as needed. Due to the nature of the lift, spotting at the implement is the safest and most effective option.

questions.question: Range of motion limitations during overhead pressing movements are most likely caused by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Tight latissimus dorsi limit the ability of the shoulder to attain full functional ranges over head. Tight lats can impact both shoulder flexion and abduction. Since the lat acts on the humerus in both the sagittal and front plane it affects military press and dumbbell presses alike.

questions.question: Which of the following exercises should not be performed due to the potential to cause serious injury?
questions.answer: Barbell box squats using a stability ball instead of a box
wrong: Dumbbell shoulder press on a physioball
Leg curls using a stability ball
Push-ups using a stability ball instead of a bench
questions.explanation: A stability ball can be used for a number of exercises but should not be used with back squats in place of another utility. The back squat is already a relatively difficult exercise and if the ball were to move or pop, the exerciser could be severely injured. As an exercise professional it is your job to assess the risk-benefit ratio of any activity. Any action that places the client at risk for injury with a missed repetition or movement of equipment – such as a supine leg press using a Smith machine – should be avoided.

questions.question: Which strengthening exercise for the pectoralis would be considered the most functional for a 65-year-old?
wrong: DB chest press off a bench
questions.explanation: Push-ups off the bench would be the most functional for this individual as it is a closed-chain activity that requires the exerciser to stabilize their core in a plank-like position while working their chest and triceps. DB or barbell bench presses are not as functional as they are open-chain exercises and stabilized. The same applies to a seated machine press, alternating or not.

questions.question: Atherosclerosis can lead to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Atherosclerosis occurs when arteries become occluded due to the build-up of fatty substances; causing the eventual reduction of blood flow to tissues. Atherosclerosis can lead to stroke, hypertension and even myocardial infarction. Obesity, smoking, high cholesterol, physical inactivity and hypertension all increase the risk of atherosclerosis.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary risk for children compared to adults during exercise?
questions.explanation: Heat injuries are a primary risk for adolescent clients due to variations in their thermoregulatory system. Adolescents are not able to produce as much sweat as adults; potentially due to smaller sweat gland sizes, lower sensitivity to heat and a lower metabolic capacity. Therefore, monitoring children for overheating during exercise, especially in hot and humid environments, is recommended.

questions.question: Which of the following can be conducted as a pre-activity assessment of risk for participation?
questions.explanation: Any measure of body composition or anthropometric evaluation can be performed as part of the pre-activity screening. BMI over 35, high central girth (males >40 inches, females >35 inches) and stage 2 or 3 obesity may warrant medical clearance.

questions.question: What is the correct course of action for an individual who cannot reach the initial measure on the sit-and-reach test to identify an actual score?
questions.answer: Document the individual as inflexible and ensure the program addresses the issue
wrong: Stretch the client's hamstrings and low back before a second attempt
Recommend the client see a chiropractor to evaluate their back
Recommend the client see a physical therapist to address any soft tissue issues
questions.explanation: It is not uncommon for people to have poor scores in the fitness-related component of fitness. Flexibility of the hamstrings and low back are often at very low levels among the general population. Failing the sit-and-reach means the client is inflexible in the posterior kinetic chain. They need to stretch, not see a physician. Document starting points and begin a client-specific approach to address the issue.

questions.question: The transverse abdominis serves which of the following purposes?
questions.explanation: The transverse abdominis is a primary deep spinal stabilizer which helps protect the spine during loaded conditions. It also helps maintain proper posture and manage forces which traverse the kinetic chain during activity. The rectus abdominis and erector spinae regulate trunk flexion and extension, respectively while the obliques laterally flex the spine.

questions.question: The sliding filament theory relates to which of the following?
questions.answer: Thin actin filaments move over the thick myosin filaments during a muscle contraction
wrong: Type II muscle fibers must interlock with type I muscle fibers for a contraction to occur
Muscle contractions are facilitated by the largest fibers first
Fiber filaments must slide down the T-tubules to facilitate a contraction
questions.explanation: Sliding filament theory cascade of events: (1) an action potential travels down T-tubules to stimulate the sarcoplasmic reticulum to release calcium ions which unlock the bond between actin filaments and troponin; (2) when troponin is moved due to calcium activity, myosin can attach to actin - forming a cross bridge via its myosin head; and (3) ATP is split at the myosin-actin attachment site which releases energy and allows the myosin head to pull or “slide” the actin to shorten/contract the muscle fiber.

questions.question: A posterior pelvic tilt should be maintained during which of the following exercises?
questions.explanation: Abdominal crunches require a posterior pelvic tilt for proper muscle activation of the rectus abdominis. An anterior or neutral tilt can allow for significant contribution from the hip flexors. Posterior pelvic tilting during the other exercise would not be appropriate for protecting the spine, especially during the RDL.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common error during stability ball leg curls?
questions.answer: Maintaining hip flexion during the concentric phase
wrong: Drawing the heels all the way to the glutes during the concentric phase
Keeping the trunk off the ground at all times during the set
Leaving the arms by the sides instead of across the chest
questions.explanation: During stability ball leg curls, the hips should be fully extended while drawing the heels to the glutes during the concentric phase. This allows for maximal activation of the hamstrings, as intended by the exercise to create force through hip extension and knee flexion at the same time.

questions.question: Which of the following is used to measure flexibility?
questions.explanation: All of the above can be used to measure flexibility. The sit-and-reach box is a common device but has limited validity based on several factors. The goniometer and inclinometer are devices used to directly measure joint angles. Both are more common in rehab/therapy settings to help identify range of motion deficits which can potentially contribute to injury. While valid and reliable to assess flexibility, successful readings are determined by protocol strictness and technician expertise.

questions.question: Which of the following training systems involves performing 2-3 sets of the same exercise in a row with transitional rest?
questions.explanation: Drop sets or strip sets on machines are used for hypertrophy and maximizing motor unit recruitment within the targeted muscle. 2-3 sets of the same exercise are commonly performed in a row - with only the time it takes to drop or “strip” the weight down serving as transitional rest between efforts. Each set is performed for a designated number of reps or volitional failure.

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning periodization is correct?
questions.answer: All of the above are correct
wrong: It uses phases of training for systemic adaptations
It identifies that the body adapts more efficiently when adaptations are strategically built upon each other
It reflects a logical method of organizing training into cyclical time periods
questions.explanation: Periodization represents the concept that the body adapts more efficiently when adaptations are strategically built upon each other. It reflects a logical method of organizing training into sequential phases and cyclical time periods. This allows adaptation residuals to be strategically built upon each other for maximal gains over time. Desired adaptive responses associated with training are referred to as supercompensation - the supercompensation curve reflects a period of time when work capacity/performance is acutely elevated. Periodization organizes phases so that these effects are built upon each other.

questions.question: Which of the following represents correct exercise order when all implemented within a strength training session?
questions.answer: Back squat, leg press, machine prone leg curl, single-leg calf raises
wrong: Machine prone leg curl, leg press, back squat, single-leg calf raises
Leg press, single-leg calf raises, back squat, machine prone leg curl
Single-leg calf raises, back squat, machine prone leg curl, leg press
questions.explanation: This selection provides the exercises in the proper order based on the intensity of the movement, loading possible for a strength phase, total volume of muscle mass used, and complexity of the action.

questions.question: Which of the following would be implemented first within a training session, assuming all are performed at 75% of 1RM?
questions.explanation: The barbell front squat would be implemented first due to the loading possible, the intensity of the movement, the stability requirements and the total musculature involved.
